confidence.disclosure: Abu Bakr is documented in the same sources as the Prophet for all the years he spent at his side. His caliphate years (632-634), though brief, are unusually well attested because al-Tabari treats the Ridda Wars in detail — including the two campaigns Abu Bakr personally led at Dhu Qissa and Abraq. His pre-Islamic decades rely on traditional reports of his standing as a Meccan merchant.

journey.event: Born in Mecca into the Banu Taym clan of the Quraysh tribe, approximately two years after the Year of the Elephant. His father was Uthman ibn Amir (Abu Quhafa) and his mother Salma bint Sakhr (Umm al-Khayr). He was given the name Abd al-Ka'ba at birth, later changed to Abdullah.

Established himself as a prosperous cloth merchant in Mecca, accumulating a fortune reported at 40,000 dirhams. He was known for his integrity, generosity, and deep knowledge of Quraysh genealogy, which made him one of the most respected notables of the city.

Travelled north to Bosra in the Syrian Hauran on a trading caravan, a major entrepot for Arabian merchants dealing in cloth, leather, and perfumes. These journeys to Byzantine Syria gave him exposure to diverse cultures and Christian communities along the caravan routes.

Undertook a trading journey south to Yemen, where Meccan merchants exchanged goods with the markets of Sana'a. Ibn Sa'd records that Abu Bakr conducted multiple commercial ventures to both Yemen and Syria, reinforcing his reputation as one of Mecca's leading traders.

Accepted Islam immediately upon hearing the Prophet's message, becoming the first free adult male to embrace the new faith. His instant belief without hesitation earned him the title al-Siddiq (the Truthful). The Prophet himself said, 'I never invited anyone to Islam who did not show some reluctance — except Abu Bakr.'
journey.source: Ibn Ishaq; Sahih al-Bukhari; Ibn Hisham

journey.event: Used his social standing and wide network among the Quraysh to bring several prominent figures to Islam, including Uthman ibn Affan, al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Talhah ibn Ubaydullah — five of the ten promised Paradise. His personal credibility was instrumental in the earliest growth of the Muslim community.

Severely beaten by Utba ibn Rabi'ah and other Quraysh opponents after publicly defending the Prophet near the Ka'ba. He was struck so viciously in the face that his nose bled profusely and he had to be carried home unconscious by members of Banu Taym. His family feared for his life for several days.

Purchased and freed Bilal ibn Rabah, an Abyssinian slave who was being tortured by his master Umayyah ibn Khalaf for embracing Islam. Abu Bakr spent a large sum from his personal wealth to manumit Bilal and several other enslaved Muslims, including Amir ibn Fuhayra and Umm Ubays.
journey.source: Ibn Ishaq; Sahih al-Bukhari; Ibn Hisham

journey.event: Endured the three-year boycott imposed by the Quraysh against Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib, during which the clans were confined to a narrow valley and cut off from trade and marriage. Although Abu Bakr belonged to Banu Taym, he chose to share the hardship, smuggling food and supplies to the besieged Muslims.

Accompanied the Prophet on his journey to Ta'if to seek support from the Thaqif tribe after the deaths of Khadijah and Abu Talib. The people of Ta'if rejected the message and pelted the Prophet with stones. Abu Bakr stayed at his side throughout the ordeal and tended to his injuries on the return to Mecca.

Helped facilitate the Pledges of Aqaba, in which delegations from the Aws and Khazraj tribes of Yathrib (Medina) secretly met the Prophet at Mina during the pilgrimage season and pledged their allegiance. These pledges laid the political groundwork for the Hijra by guaranteeing the Muslims a safe haven.

Departed Mecca with the Prophet on the Hijra, hiding for three days in the Cave of Thawr on Mount Thawr south of Mecca while Quraysh search parties scoured the area. The Quran references this moment: 'When they were in the cave, he said to his companion, Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us' (9:40). Abu Bakr's daughter Asma brought them provisions each night.

Arrived at Quba on the outskirts of Yathrib after a journey of roughly eight days via the coastal route to avoid Quraysh pursuers. The Prophet and Abu Bakr stayed at Quba for several days, during which the Mosque of Quba — the first mosque in Islam — was established.

Settled in the al-Sunh quarter of Medina among the Banu al-Harith ibn al-Khazraj. He established himself as one of the Prophet's closest advisors, participating in the drafting of the Constitution of Medina that regulated relations between the Muhajirun, Ansar, and Jewish tribes of the city.

Fought at the Battle of Badr, the first major military engagement between the Muslims and the Quraysh. Abu Bakr served in the Prophet's personal guard at the command post, and his son Abd al-Rahman fought on the opposing Quraysh side. The Muslim victory against a numerically superior force established the community's military credibility.

Fought at the Battle of Uhud, where the Muslim forces initially gained the upper hand but suffered a reversal when archers abandoned their position. Abu Bakr was among the small group of companions who stood firm around the wounded Prophet when the Quraysh cavalry counter-attacked, shielding him from further harm.

Participated in the Battle of the Trench (al-Khandaq), where a coalition of Quraysh, Ghatafan, and other tribes besieged Medina. Abu Bakr commanded a section of the trench defence on the northern perimeter. The siege lasted nearly a month before the coalition forces withdrew, broken by storms and the defection of their allies.

Present at the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, where the Prophet negotiated a ten-year truce with the Quraysh. When some companions objected to the apparently unfavorable terms, Abu Bakr urged them to trust the Prophet's judgement, telling Umar, 'Hold fast to his stirrup, for by Allah he is right.'

Entered Mecca with the Muslim army during the Conquest of Mecca (Fath Makkah), in which the city was taken almost without bloodshed. Abu Bakr brought his elderly father Abu Quhafa, who was nearly blind, before the Prophet to formally declare his acceptance of Islam. The Prophet gently told Abu Bakr he should have let the old man stay home.

Fought at the Battle of Hunayn against the Hawazin and Thaqif confederacy shortly after the Conquest of Mecca. When an ambush in the narrow valley caused the Muslim vanguard to scatter, Abu Bakr was among those who remained with the Prophet until reinforcements rallied and turned the battle into a decisive victory.

Accompanied the Prophet on the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada'), the only Hajj the Prophet performed after the conquest of Mecca. Standing at Arafat, the Prophet delivered his final sermon to over 100,000 pilgrims. Abu Bakr had led the Hajj the previous year (631) as the Prophet's appointed representative.
journey.source: Sahih al-Bukhari; Sahih Muslim; Ibn Ishaq

journey.event: Elected as the first caliph (Khalifat Rasul Allah) at the Saqifah of Banu Sa'ida, a covered meeting place in Medina, on the day of the Prophet's death. After intense deliberation between the Muhajirun and Ansar, Umar ibn al-Khattab pledged allegiance to Abu Bakr, and the assembly followed. He addressed the Muslims: 'If I do well, help me; if I do wrong, set me right.'

Personally led the Muslim army to Dhu Qissa, east of Medina, to confront the apostate forces of Kharijah ibn Hisn al-Fazari and Tulayha's advance guard during the Ridda Wars. Despite counsel from companions to remain in Medina, Abu Bakr insisted on commanding the first counter-attack himself, driving the rebels back from the approaches to the capital.

Led a second expedition to Abraq al-Rabadha, pursuing the retreating rebel forces deeper into the Najd. After securing this position, he entrusted further operations to his generals — most notably Khalid ibn al-Walid — and returned to Medina to coordinate the broader campaign against apostasy across Arabia.

Dispatched multiple armies to conquer the frontiers of the Byzantine and Sasanian empires, ordering Khalid ibn al-Walid to Iraq and Amr ibn al-As, Yazid ibn Abi Sufyan, Shurahbil ibn Hasana, and Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah to Syria. These campaigns marked the beginning of the great Arab conquests that would reshape the map of the ancient world.

Died in Medina on 22 Jumada al-Thani 13 AH after a brief illness of approximately fifteen days. He nominated Umar ibn al-Khattab as his successor after consulting senior companions. He was buried beside the Prophet in the chamber of Aisha, his daughter, in what is now part of the Prophet's Mosque (al-Masjid al-Nabawi).
